title: A56
abstract_summary: This work analyzes the formation of channel segregates in castings, emphasizing the role of small defects in the dendrite network. The analysis reexamines the limitations of existing criteria (e.g., [1]) and introduces an improved condition [10] that accounts for defect permeability. It concludes that defects significantly assist channel segregation by diverting interdendritic flow, even when flow is nearly parallel to isotherms.
research_objective: To investigate how small defects in the dendrite network promote channel segregation and to establish a more reliable criterion for its occurrence in real castings.
key_sections: Introduction: background on channel segregation and existing criteria [1] and [10]
Model derivation: formulation of defect-permeability based criterion and percolation model considerations
Discussion: limitations of model assumptions (ellipsoidal defect, constant n, high liquid fraction accuracy)
Conclusions: defect-assisted growth, improved criterion, and flow parallel to isotherms can still lead to segregation
